title: INSARAG / UNDAC SAR fundamentals — corpus-grounded curriculum

modules.summary: United Nations Disaster Assessment and Coordination (UNDAC) team operations follow three interrelated phases: pre-mission, on-mission, and mission-end. On deployment, UNDAC may stand up two coordination centres: OSOCC (On-Site Operations Coordination Centre) and RDC (Reception Departure Centre). Grounded in the UNDAC Field Handbook (2022). 789 corpus chunks support this topic — the highest-coverage subject in the knowledge base.

case_studies.text: M7.2 earthquake strikes a coastal city in Hispaniola at 02:14 local. Government issues a request for international assistance within 4 hours. You are an UNDAC team member on the global roster. Activation message arrives at 06:00. Wheels-up target is 24 hours. Pre-mission phase: passport + vaccinations current, go-bag ready, family notified, briefing scheduled with OCHA Geneva.
key_points: Pre-mission phase = activation + travel preparation. Closes when wheels-up.
OCHA Geneva briefs the team with situation report, host-state context, security level.
Personal readiness items (passport, vaccinations, INSARAG ID card) must be current at all times — verified during alert, not deployment.
Travel orders, per-diem, and visas handled by OCHA — UNDAC member focuses on operational readiness.

case_studies.text: First UNDAC element lands at SDQ (Las Américas) 26 hours post-quake. Three INSARAG-classified Heavy USAR teams en route. Local government cooperative but overwhelmed. Team Leader decides to stand up an RDC at the airport BEFORE convoy to OSOCC site, because international teams will start arriving within 6 hours. RDC handles team registration, customs, briefing, then forwards to OSOCC for tasking.
key_points: RDC is the first-touch point for arriving international teams — at airport or port of entry.
OSOCC coordinates on-site response: tasking, sectorisation, daily briefings.
Both centres must establish a daily-briefing rhythm and shared information protocol (typically morning brief + evening sitrep).
RDC support role requires logistics expertise (manifests, IATA dangerous-goods, customs), not just registration.
On-mission phase closes when host-state lead is restored or the situation no longer warrants UN coordination.

quiz_seeds.prompt: What are the three interrelated phases of an UNDAC mission cycle?
quiz_seeds.explanation: UNDAC Field Handbook §3.1 defines the cycle as pre-mission (alert + readiness), on-mission (in-country coordination), and mission-end (handover + after-action). 'Activation/Deployment/Demob' is the INSARAG USAR cycle — different concept.

quiz_seeds.prompt: Which UNDAC-established centre is the FIRST-touch point for arriving international USAR teams?
quiz_seeds.explanation: The RDC is established at the port of entry (airport or land border) by the first-arriving international personnel — often USAR, EMT, or UNDAC members. It records incoming teams, handles customs/visas, then forwards them to the OSOCC for tasking.

quiz_seeds.prompt: During an UNDAC mission, the Assessment & Analysis (A&A) function delivers outputs across phases. The Phase I priority outputs are:
quiz_seeds.explanation: Phase I (first 72 hrs) outputs are situation analysis and exposure estimates. Caseload estimations and humanitarian profile come in Phase II. The recovery framework is later. Source: UNDAC Field Handbook p.115.

modules.summary: INSARAG External Classification (IEC) ranks international USAR teams by structural capability: Light handles wood, masonry, and light reinforced concrete; Medium adds heavy wood, lightweight steel, and reinforced masonry; Heavy adds heavy-wall reinforced concrete and steel structures. Classification governs the type of collapse a team is authorised to operate on and drives task allocation at the OSOCC.

case_studies.text: OSOCC receives a request for tasking on a 6-storey reinforced-concrete office tower with progressive pancake collapse. Three teams are available: a Light team (just landed), a Medium team (already on a different site, can rotate), and a Heavy team (in convoy, ETA 90 min). The Sector Coordinator must choose.
key_points: Light USAR is NOT authorised for heavy-wall reinforced concrete — collapse type exceeds capability.
Medium USAR can intervene on reinforced masonry and lightweight steel but is at the edge for heavy reinforced concrete.
Heavy USAR is the appropriate match for heavy-wall RC and steel structures.
Correct decision: hold the Light team for lighter-construction sites; let Medium stabilise/recon while Heavy convoys in.
Always match classification to structure type — mismatch risks responder casualties + secondary collapse.

quiz_seeds.prompt: A Heavy USAR team is REQUIRED (not just preferred) when the collapse involves:
options: Heavy-wall reinforced concrete or steel structures
quiz_seeds.explanation: Heavy USAR classification mandates capability on heavy-wall reinforced concrete and steel — Medium and Light teams are NOT authorised on these structures. INSARAG Guidelines Vol II Manual A defines the structural-capability matrix.

quiz_seeds.prompt: A Light USAR team's authorised structural scope includes:
options: Wood, masonry, and light reinforced concrete only
All collapse types — Light refers to deployment speed, not capability
quiz_seeds.explanation: Light USAR is bounded to wood, masonry, and LIGHT reinforced concrete. Heavy wood and reinforced masonry require Medium classification. 'Light' refers to structural-capability ceiling, not response speed.

modules.summary: Simple Triage And Rapid Treatment. 4-color casualty classification used at multi-casualty incidents: RED (immediate), YELLOW (delayed), GREEN (walking wounded), BLACK (deceased or expectant). 213 corpus chunks cover triage methods (START + SALT).

case_studies.text: Bus rollover, 12 casualties. First-arriving SAR team. Two responders. You have 6 minutes to triage the entire scene before more help arrives.
key_points: anyone walking is GREEN — direct to a fixed location
non-walking: assess respiration first. No breathing after airway open = BLACK
respiration > 30 breaths/min = RED
no radial pulse OR capillary refill > 2 sec = RED
altered mental status (cannot follow simple command) = RED

quiz_seeds.prompt: In START triage, an adult with respirations of 36/min should be classified:
quiz_seeds.explanation: RR > 30 in adults triggers RED in START regardless of perfusion or mental status.

quiz_seeds.prompt: A casualty who walks to you complaining of arm pain is classified:
quiz_seeds.explanation: Walking wounded are GREEN by definition in START. Move them to a fixed location and re-assess only if scene allows.

modules.summary: Eight SAR hazard categories used in Kognis Lite marker types: VICTIM, MEDICAL, HAZARD (structural · chemical · fire · flood), COMMAND, EXTRACTION, MISSING, BASE, WATER. Correct classification at the marker level drives downstream resource allocation. 244 corpus chunks cover hazard identification.

case_studies.text: Industrial facility post-flood. Operator sees a chemical smell, eye irritation, several rusted drums leaking unknown fluid. Wind direction is variable. Three civilians 50m downwind.
key_points: mark as HAZARD (chemical sub-type)
stand-off distance based on visible plume + symptoms — minimum 30m
evacuate downwind civilians FIRST
report to ICP with wind direction + estimated plume size

quiz_seeds.prompt: A partially collapsed bridge structure should be marked as which Kognis marker type?
quiz_seeds.explanation: Structural compromise = HAZARD. EXTRACTION is reserved for helicopter LZs and evacuation points.

modules.summary: SITREP structure, callsign discipline, brevity codes used by INSARAG / UNDAC field teams. Standard formats prevent ambiguity under stress, low signal, multilingual teams. 214 corpus chunks support comms training.

case_studies.text: Sector lead reports to ICP after the first hour on scene. They have completed initial survey of 3 collapse sites, identified 2 confirmed survivors with audible response, 4 missing persons reported by family, and 1 active chemical leak 200m east.
key_points: SITREP format: location · situation · activity · request
use phonetic alphabet for site IDs
absolute coordinates over relative descriptions when reporting hazards
request EXPLICIT acknowledgement before signing off

quiz_seeds.prompt: In a SITREP, the correct order of elements is:
quiz_seeds.explanation: Location-Situation-Activity-Request (LSAR) is the INSARAG-standard ordering. Location FIRST anchors the report; request LAST so the receiver has full context before deciding.

modules.summary: ITS MARCH — primary-survey mnemonic for tactical trauma: Immediate Threats · Tourniquet/Tactical environment · Stop bleeding · Massive hemorrhage · Airway · Respiration · Circulation · Head/Hypothermia. Combat-derived (TECC/TCCC/KATT) protocol used by INSARAG medical teams for crush injuries, complex extrications, and field trauma. 22 corpus chunks (MARCH-TECC-TCCC-KATT pocket guide) ground this module.

case_studies.text: M6.8 earthquake. 3-story collapse. Adult male, ~40 yrs, lower-limb crush under concrete slab. Pulse 130, RR 28, conscious but pale. Distal pulse absent on the trapped leg. Estimated 4 hours of entrapment. Team has 18 minutes before extrication risk for crush syndrome increases.
key_points: Apply tourniquet proximal to the bleed site, between wound and heart
After application: distal pulse re-checked within 60 seconds; if pulse present, tighten further
Record tourniquet time on patient skin / forehead / tag
Fluid resuscitation prepared BEFORE extrication for crush-syndrome prevention
Brachial-style proximal pressure (hands around limb at midpoint) is the bridge while tourniquet is sourced

case_studies.text: Pancake collapse. Adult female, ~35 yrs, blunt chest trauma + open chest wound 4cm right hemithorax. Conscious, RR 32 laboured, SpO2 dropping. Assess using FLASH then seal.
key_points: FLASH chest mnemonic: Feel (deformity / crepitus) · Look (paradox / open wound) · Armpits · Search the back · Holes
Open chest wound: seal IMMEDIATELY with hand first, then commercial occlusive (chest seal)
If respiratory status worsens AFTER seal applied → suspect tension pneumothorax → BURP the dressing (briefly lift to release pressure)
Position for max respiratory effort (sitting / lateral with injured side down per local protocol)
Reassess continuously — every 5 min minimum for chest trauma

case_studies.text: Building fire, secondary collapse. Adult male, ~25, partial-thickness burns covering right arm, chest, and abdomen. Conscious. Smell of accelerant. Clothing still smouldering.
key_points: STOP the burning process FIRST — smother / remove from heat source
Cool affected area with water for ≥10 min (longer for chemical burns)
Remove loose burnt clothing + jewellery (rings, watches) BEFORE swelling sets in
Estimate TBSA using Rule of Palms: casualty's own palm including fingers ≈ 1% TBSA
Cover with light clean dressing AFTER cooling; lay flat + keep warm to manage shock

quiz_seeds.prompt: The tactical-environment primary-survey mnemonic taught in the MARCH pocket guide is:
quiz_seeds.explanation: ITS MARCH is the structured primary-survey protocol used in tactical / field trauma. ABCDE is the civilian-trauma equivalent; SAMPLE is a history mnemonic; AVPU is a consciousness scale. (Source: MARCH-TECC-TCCC-KATT pocket guide p.3)

quiz_seeds.prompt: In MARCH, what is the FIRST action for a catastrophic limb hemorrhage?
quiz_seeds.explanation: Massive bleeding kills fastest. Tourniquet placement BEFORE airway is the MARCH ordering — preventable mortality is dominated by uncontrolled hemorrhage in field trauma.

quiz_seeds.prompt: After tourniquet application, what time-critical action is required?
options: Record application time on the patient
Cover the tourniquet to reduce shock
Replace with a pressure dressing within 5 minutes
quiz_seeds.explanation: The receiving team needs the application time to decide on extended-care management. Never loosen a tourniquet in the field without surgical capability.

quiz_seeds.prompt: The FLASH mnemonic for chest-trauma assessment stands for:
options: Feel · Look · Armpits · Search the back · Holes
quiz_seeds.explanation: FLASH is the structured chest-assessment protocol: Feel (deformity/crepitus), Look (paradox/wound), Armpits (occult wounds), Search the back (entry/exit), Holes (open wounds). Pocket guide p.10.

quiz_seeds.prompt: After applying an occlusive chest seal, the casualty's breathing WORSENS. The next action is:
options: Apply a second seal alongside the first
Burp the dressing — briefly lift one corner to release built-up pressure
Remove the seal completely and pack the wound
quiz_seeds.explanation: Worsening respiration after seal application suggests tension pneumothorax developing under the seal. BURPING (briefly lifting to vent) releases the pressure without losing the seal. Pocket guide p.11.

quiz_seeds.prompt: Using the Rule of Palms for burn assessment, the casualty's own palm INCLUDING the fingers represents approximately:
quiz_seeds.explanation: One palm including fingers ≈ 1% Total Body Surface Area. Fast on-scene estimation method. Pocket guide p.16.

quiz_seeds.prompt: The standard mnemonic for casualty handover to receiving medical care is:
quiz_seeds.explanation: ATMIST-Vital Signs: Age · Time of incident · Mechanism · Injuries · Signs/symptoms · Treatment given · Vital signs. Standardised across UK/EU prehospital and INSARAG medical teams. Pocket guide p.17.
